reasoning_excerpt: Sprewell explicitly holds courts need not accept allegations contradicted by exhibits/documents referenced in the complaint, directly supporting the proposition.
Bowie actually criticizes and rejects the broad 'intrinsic acts' exception to Rule 404(b), holding the rule is not bypassed merely because evidence is inextricably intertwined with the charged crime.
The opinion contains the proposition nearly verbatim in paragraph 17, directly supporting the claim about Congress's broad power over immigration and naturalization.
The excerpt addresses patent surrender/reissue procedure and the Secretary of State's authority, not the constitutional policy rationale for granting Congress power over exclusive privileges.

Gaus holds removal statutes are strictly construed and doubts about removal defeat federal jurisdiction, which implies favoring remand, but it does not use the specific 'resolve any ambiguities in favor of remand' formulation.

Durham addresses abatement when a certiorari petition (not a direct appeal) is pending at death, and explicitly extends the rule beyond direct appeals, so it supports the broader abatement doctrine but does not specifically stand for the narrower 'direct appeal pending' formulation.

The court holds that once counsel was retained by the corporation, any privilege belonged to the corporation, not its officers, directly supporting the proposition that corporate counsel represents the entity rather than individual officers/directors.
The quoted proposition is verbatim from the opinion and accurately reflects the Court's holding that personal jurisdiction is an individual right that can be waived.
Leon quotes Janis that the exclusionary rule's use is unwarranted absent appreciable deterrence and applies cost-benefit balancing tied to deterrence throughout.
Herring expressly states the exclusionary rule 'applies only where it result[s] in appreciable deterrence,' directly supporting the proposition.
Tison addresses Eighth Amendment proportionality of the death penalty for non-triggerman felony-murder accomplices, not the historical entrenchment of the felony murder rule itself.

The case discusses district court discretion over supersedeas bonds but emphasizes the usual requirement of a full bond and places the burden on the movant to justify departure, so it only partially supports the broad proposition of unfettered discretion.

The opinion discusses patent rights and the right to exclude in the contributory infringement context, but the excerpt does not articulate the broad proposition that the essential attribute of a patent is the right to exclude competitors from infringement.

Moore expressly leaves open whether the Fourth Amendment requires the offense to be committed 'in the presence' of the officer, noting that the Court has only addressed in-presence arrests and need not decide otherwise.
The excerpt addresses whether a threat made in jest is punishable, not whether a conditional threat qualifies as a threat under the statute.
The excerpt addresses Rule 403 balancing and stipulation to a prior conviction; it does not discuss limiting the Government's elicited details plus a precautionary limiting instruction as safeguards.
Iqbal is a Rule 12(b)(6) motion-to-dismiss case in which the Supreme Court evaluated the sufficiency of the complaint, directly supporting the proposition.
The opinion contains the exact quoted language about no legal basis for retaining the fine and addresses only the paid fine and special assessment, with no restitution having been ordered.

Blackledge v. Perry establishes the prosecutorial vindictiveness doctrine, holding due process bars prosecutors from penalizing a defendant for invoking a legally protected right (there, statutory right to appeal).
The proposition is a near-verbatim quote of the Court's holding in Smith v. Spizzirri.
Wallace v. Kato expressly holds that the accrual date of a Â§ 1983 cause of action is a question of federal law, directly supporting the proposition.
Winter expressly lists likelihood of success on the merits as one of the four factors a plaintiff must establish to obtain a preliminary injunction.

Cervantes affirms dismissal without leave to amend on futility grounds, but the futility there concerned fraud/MERS pleading deficiencies rather than a time-barred claim, so it only loosely supports the specific proposition about time-bar futility.

The opinion expressly holds that 'serious questions going to the merits' and a hardship balance tipping sharply toward the plaintiff can support an injunction when the other two Winter elements are met.
The opinion explicitly states the quoted standard, holding that a district court should grant leave to amend unless the pleading could not possibly be cured by the allegation of other facts.
Iqbal directly contains the quoted language about threadbare recitals and legal conclusions couched as factual allegations not being accepted as true.
The opinion contains the exact quoted language stating that absent an applicable federal statute, the district court applies the law of the state in which it sits.
Daimler explicitly states that the paradigm all-purpose forums for general jurisdiction over a corporation are its place of incorporation and principal place of business.
The opinion expressly states federal courts may take notice of proceedings in other courts if those proceedings have a direct relation to matters at issue, matching the proposition verbatim.
The case discusses the federal discovery rule for civil rights claim accrual, not California's discovery rule of tolling as the proposition asserts.

Muldrow confirms Title VII covers terms/conditions discrimination but requires showing some harm to an identifiable term/condition; the proposition omits this harm requirement and overstates the holding.

Shinseki v. Sanders holds that harmless-error review applies in VA administrative proceedings and that notice errors do not require reversal absent prejudice to substantial rights, directly supporting the proposition.
Gamble explicitly holds that disability benefits cannot be denied because a claimant cannot afford prescribed treatment or a needed medical device, directly supporting the proposition.

Brock applies Anderson/Mt. Clemens Pottery to hold that employees' testimony about hours worked, based on personal knowledge, was sufficient evidence to support a back-wages award where employer records were inadequate.
The opinion expressly quotes this exact language from Heiser v. Woodruff to reject creating equitable exceptions to res judicata, directly supporting the proposition.

Anderson holds an employee carries the burden by producing sufficient evidence to show the amount of work as a matter of just and reasonable inference, which supports treating personal-knowledge testimony of hours worked as sufficient.

Tyson Foods discusses Anderson/Mt. Clemens as allowing representative/statistical evidence to fill gaps from inadequate employer records, not specifically endorsing personal-knowledge testimony of hours worked as sufficient under Anderson.
The Court explicitly held that claimants who exhaust administrative remedies need not also exhaust issues in a request for review by the Appeals Council, directly matching the proposition.

Mt. Healthy held that an Ohio local school board is more like a city/county than an arm of the State for Eleventh Amendment purposes, but the proposition specifies a Texas independent school district, which the case did not address.
The opinion contains this exact language verbatim: 'the focus must be on adequacy of the training program in relation to the tasks the particular officers must perform.'

The Court expressly holds that for a state prisoner who does not seek review in the State's highest court, the judgment becomes final on the date the time for seeking such review expires under Â§ 2244(d)(1)(A).
The court explicitly states that a default constitutes concession of all well-pleaded liability allegations but is not an admission of damages, directly supporting the proposition.
The opinion explicitly states that Â§Â§1225(b)(1) and (b)(2) 'mandate detention of applicants for admission until certain proceedings have concluded,' directly matching the proposition.
Younger directly holds that federal courts must refrain from enjoining pending state criminal prosecutions absent extraordinary circumstances, matching the proposition.
Frank explicitly states that absent a wax-and-wane showing, the ability-to-maintain-employment issue is subsumed in the ability-to-work/RFC analysis.
Neitzke holds precisely that the Rule 12(b)(6) failure-to-state-a-claim standard and the Â§1915(d) frivolousness standard are distinct and should not be conflated.
The case explicitly states that under Local Rule 56.1(c), if the opposing party fails to controvert a fact in the moving party's Rule 56.1 statement, that fact will be deemed admitted.
Heller holds that a municipality cannot be liable under Monell when the officer inflicted no constitutional injury, directly supporting the proposition.

Celotex addresses summary judgment burdens generally but does not specifically articulate the local-rule concept that unopposed facts are deemed admitted; it supports the underlying principle only indirectly.
Gibbs explicitly states that if federal claims are dismissed before trial, the state claims should be dismissed as well, directly supporting the proposition.
The Greeno excerpt does not contain the quoted Collignon language about deference to medical professionals; the proposition's quoted standard is not articulated in this excerpt.
The Second Circuit reversed the 12(b)(6) dismissal of the contract claim, holding that ambiguity in the CDS contracts could not be resolved at the pleading stage and required further proceedings.

Chance recognizes that mere disagreement over treatment isn't a constitutional violation, but it doesn't use the 'range of acceptable courses' phrasing and actually emphasizes that choosing an easier/less efficacious treatment can constitute deliberate indifference.
The proposition quotes language directly from the closing paragraph of the opinion regarding the ALJ's obligation to consider the aggregate effect of severe and non-severe impairments.
The opinion expressly states the third standing element requires it be 'likely,' as opposed to merely 'speculative,' that the injury will be redressed by a favorable decision.

Mitchell requires similarly-situated comparators to share the same supervisor, standards, and conduct, but the opinion excerpt does not specifically articulate the 'similar disciplinary history and performance record' formulation in the proposition.
The case contrasts Rule 12(b)(6) dismissal with Â§1915(d) frivolousness dismissal, not motion to dismiss versus summary judgment standards.

The Ninth Circuit held that labeling an inmate a 'snitch' to subject him to inmate retaliation states a Â§ 1983 claim for violation of the right to be protected from violence, directly supporting the proposition.

The case approves sua sponte action on venue (dismissal under Â§1406(a)) and analogizes to sua sponte Â§1404(a) transfers, but it does not directly hold that a court can sua sponte order a transfer absent a motion.

Opoka expressly holds that federal courts may take judicial notice of proceedings in other courts when those proceedings have a direct relation to matters at issue, directly supporting the proposition.
Lujan establishes Article III standing's injury-in-fact requirement, which embodies the colloquial 'what's it to you?' question about a plaintiff's stake.
The opinion expressly states that on a 12(b)(6) motion the court may consider documents incorporated into the complaint, matters of public record, and facts susceptible to judicial notice.

The Quaker State opinion expressly cites Whitinsville Plaza, Inc. v. Kotseas, 390 N.E.2d 243, 251 (1979), for the proposition that a breach of contract, standing alone, does not constitute a chapter 93A violation, directly supporting the claim.

The case explicitly holds that federal courts may take notice of proceedings in other courts, both within and without the federal system, if those proceedings have a direct relation to matters at issue.
Lexmark reframes the zone-of-interests inquiry as statutory interpretation, not as guidance on the procedural treatment of non-jurisdictional issues.

Opoka expressly holds that federal courts may take judicial notice of proceedings in other courts where those proceedings have a direct relation to matters at issue, directly supporting the proposition.
Green directly addresses the intrinsic-evidence doctrine and holds that truly intrinsic evidence falls outside Rule 404(b)'s scope, supporting the proposition.
Hausler addresses whether mid-stream EFTs are attachable property of Cuba under TRIA Â§ 201, not whether Â§ 201 limits execution to blocked assets only; the case concerns blocked EFTs throughout.

Nautilus articulates a 'reasonable certainty' standard and does not contain the quoted language about 'unreasonable precision' or 'verbal precision' in 'a patent system that actually works'â€”that phrasing comes from other cases.

Mayo holds the oppositeâ€”that one defendant's representation in the notice of removal that a co-defendant consented is sufficient, rejecting any requirement of independent written consent from each defendant.
The case affirms Congress's constitutional power to grant exclusive patent rights but does not explicitly discuss the national policy importance that motivated giving this power to Congress.

The case states doubts about jurisdiction should be resolved in favor of the states/remand, but uses different phrasing than the quoted language about 'ambiguities,' so support is close but not verbatim.
The excerpt is only an errata notice correcting a typo and contains no substantive discussion of irreparable harm or marketing exclusivity.

Atwater addressed whether warrantless arrests for fine-only misdemeanors violate the Fourth Amendment and did not reserve the 'in the presence' question; the excerpt shows the Court resolving the case on probable cause grounds without reserving that issue.
The court explicitly applies the entity rule, holding that a lawyer retained by a corporation represents the entity, not its shareholders or constituents.

Gibbs is the foundational pendent/supplemental jurisdiction case holding federal courts may hear state claims arising from a 'common nucleus of operative fact' as the federal claim, which directly supports exercising supplemental jurisdiction over state claims arising from the same facts as RICO claims.
Atwater addresses warrantless arrests for minor misdemeanors under the Fourth Amendment, not a circuit-court consensus on whether offenses must be committed in the officer's presence.

The opinion explicitly states federal law determines when a Â§ 1983 cause of action accrues and that accrual occurs when the plaintiff knows or has reason to know of the injury, directly supporting the proposition.
The excerpt addresses CWA and RCRA citizen suits, mootness, and standing â€” it contains no discussion of the relationship between hazardous wastes and solid wastes.
The excerpt addresses Medicaid fraud, indictment sufficiency, and duplicityâ€”not the rule against considering issues raised for the first time at oral argument.
The excerpt addresses tort liability in the interpleader context, not the statute of limitations for abuse of process claims.
Fox expressly states the discovery rule 'postpones accrual of a cause of action until the plaintiff discovers, or has reason to discover, the cause of action,' directly supporting the proposition.
This case concerns California's anti-SLAPP statute and limited-purpose public figures in defamation, not Rule 15(d) supplemental pleadings.

The opinion explicitly states 'Where, as here, no federal statute authorizes personal jurisdiction, the district court applies the law of the state in which the court sits,' directly supporting the proposition.
The opinion explains that before a material can be designated as 'hazardous waste,' it must first be a 'solid waste,' confirming that hazardous wastes are a subset of solid wastes.
Adams holds plaintiffs must bring all claims arising from the same transactional nucleus in one suit and authorizes dismissal of duplicative later-filed actions, directly supporting the proposition.
The case discusses waiver of issues raised for the first time in a reply brief, not issues raised for the first time during oral argument.

The excerpt shows Kuebel involves off-the-clock FLSA claims and references the Anderson burden-shifting framework context, but the provided text does not contain the specific holding that personal-knowledge testimony of hours worked is sufficient evidence under Anderson.
The Eleventh Circuit explicitly held that a claimant's inability to afford prescribed treatment excuses noncompliance, meaning the otherwise-remediable condition remains disabling.
The quoted language is taken verbatim from Heiser v. Woodruff, where the Supreme Court held the bankruptcy court could not disregard res judicata.
The excerpt addresses the FLSA predominant-benefit standard for meal-break compensability and does not discuss Anderson v. Mt. Clemens or the sufficiency of employee testimony about hours worked.
The court directly applies 28 U.S.C. Â§ 2675(b) and holds that an FTCA award cannot exceed the administrative claim amount absent newly discovered evidence or intervening facts.
Brown v. Ahern holds that Younger abstention principles bar pre-conviction habeas relief absent extraordinary circumstances, directly supporting the proposition.
The Second Circuit holds local school boards are municipal bodies, not arms of the state, and thus subject to suit in federal court under the Eleventh Amendment, directly supporting the proposition.
Rodriguez addresses equal protection challenges to Texas school financing, not Eleventh Amendment immunity or whether school districts are arms of the state.
The case discusses pre-trial habeas exhaustion and abstention under Braden/Royall, not Younger abstention as a prerequisite to habeas relief.
The opinion explicitly states the general rule that upon default the factual allegations of the complaint, except those relating to damages, are taken as true.

Maleng is a Supreme Court (not Fifth Circuit) case interpreting habeas 'in custody' broadly to include non-physical restraints like parole, which loosely supports the proposition's substance but not its attribution to the Fifth Circuit.
Hicks extends Younger to require federal abstention from enjoining state criminal proceedings begun before substantial federal proceedings, directly supporting the proposition.
The excerpt does not mention Singletary or any specific determination required by it; the case addresses medical improvement standards and evidence consideration, not the Singletary issue.

The Fifth Circuit in Pack expressly states in footnote 5 that '[u]sually, custody signifies incarceration or supervised release, but in general it encompasses most restrictions on liberty resulting from a criminal conviction,' directly matching the proposition.
The case discusses the standard for IFP appeals and notes that frivolous appeals can be dismissed, but does not squarely hold that granting IFP while dismissing as frivolous is self-contradictory.
Meuir holds that mere differences of opinion over treatment courses don't violate the Constitution absent a substantial departure from professional judgment, directly supporting the proposition.
Anderson addresses the summary judgment standard (and its relation to directed verdict), not a contrast between motion-to-dismiss and summary-judgment standards.

The case holds an insurer must defend whenever the complaint's allegations potentially give rise to a covered claim and bears a heavy burden to show the pleadings fall wholly within an exclusion, directly supporting the proposition.

The case states an insurer's duty to defend arises when allegations could potentially result in policy liability, which aligns with the proposition's substance, but the excerpt does not use the specific 'unjustifiable refusal' framing quoted.

Scott v. Harris addresses summary judgment standards and when a court may disregard a nonmovant's version of facts contradicted by video evidence; it does not state that uncontested facts are deemed admitted for failure to create a genuine dispute.
Sanchez holds that a difference of medical opinion does not amount to deliberate indifference, directly supporting the proposition.
Flannery expressly rejects the estoppel rule, holding that an insurer's breach of the duty to defend does NOT preclude it from contesting indemnity coverageâ€”the opposite of the proposition.
The opinion expressly states that under Illinois law the duty to defend arises when the facts alleged in the underlying complaint fall within or potentially within the policy's coverage.
The court reversed because the ALJ disregarded the vocational expert's unfavorable answers to hypotheticals about climbing limitations, directly supporting the proposition.

The opinion explicitly states the general rule that when all federal claims are dismissed before trial, the district court should relinquish jurisdiction over pendent state-law claims rather than resolving them on the merits.

Sain cites Collignon and applies its deliberate-indifference framework to medical/treatment decisions for civil detainees, but the excerpt does not contain the specific 'minimally competent medical professional' deference quotation, so support is partial rather than direct.

The case supports that a mere difference of medical opinion between two medically acceptable courses doesn't show deliberate indifference, but it doesn't use the 'range of acceptable courses' language and emphasizes the treatment must be medically acceptable, not merely chosen from a range.
The excerpt addresses antitrust limitations, waiver of defenses, and procedural amendment under Rule 15(a); it does not discuss the distinction between a release and a covenant not to sue.
Pabon concerns a Fourteenth Amendment right to medical information for informed refusal of treatment; it does not hold that a doctor is not deliberately indifferent when a prisoner refuses treatment.
The case discusses motion to dismiss pleading standards and mentions summary judgment as a later mechanism to dispose of unmeritorious claims, but does not substantively contrast the two standards.
The Seventh Circuit held that an inference of causation was reasonable where Loudermilk was fired immediately after handing his supervisor a note detailing his discrimination complaint.
Tolan addresses the summary judgment standard (drawing inferences for the nonmovant) but does not contrast it with the motion to dismiss standard.

The excerpt applies Massachusetts law and discusses the continuity-of-shareholders factor, but it does not state that the factor was unsatisfied because the defendant paid cash for the assets; the case actually imposed successor liability.

The excerpt addresses excessive force and death threats under the Eighth Amendment; it does not contain any holding or discussion that labeling an inmate a snitch may violate constitutional guarantees.

The court held that because Exxon/G.E. Capital were furnishers and not consumer reporting agencies, plaintiff's allegations failed to state a claim under Â§ 1681i, directly supporting the proposition.
The excerpt addresses PLRA exhaustion and Eighth Amendment claims, not the doctrine that an amended complaint supersedes prior pleadings.

Browder holds Federal Rules of Civil Procedure (specifically 52(b) and 59) apply in habeas proceedings and references a motion to dismiss in passing, but it does not specifically address or hold that Rule 12(b)(6) motions are consistent with habeas rules.
The excerpt does not contain the quoted passage about judicial notice of other courts' proceedings; the case addresses ancillary jurisdiction over an attorneys' fee dispute, not judicial notice.
Schwarzschild addresses whether a defendant who won summary judgment can compel Rule 23(c)(2) notice, not plaintiffs seeking certification after losing; it does not support the stated proposition.
The excerpt is merely an errata sheet correcting a date and contains no substantive discussion supporting any proposition.

Hollingsworth holds Article III requires a personal, particularized injury â€” i.e., the litigant must have a personal stake distinguishable from a generalized grievance, which is the substance of the 'what's it to you?' framing.
The excerpt addresses ex post facto, due process, self-incrimination, and SOTP classification procedures, but contains no discussion of the sex offender label exposing inmates to prison violence.
The case applies the TRAC factors, including the rule of reason that depends on the complexity and nature of the agency task, directly supporting the proposition.

Wigod actually reversed dismissal of the implied covenant/contract claim, holding the bank's TPP obligations were enforceable, so it does not support dismissing such a claim for lack of obligation to modify.
The excerpt shows the case discusses CRA reinvestigation duties under FCRA/CCRAA but does not contain the specific quoted language limiting Â§ 1681i's reasonable investigation duty to CRAs.

The opinion explicitly states courts can consider implications from documents incorporated into the complaint, judicially noticeable facts, and concessions in plaintiff's response to the motion to dismiss.

The cited case is The Bremen, not Carnival Cruise Lines, and while it discusses the 'unreasonable and unjust' standard, the excerpt does not contain the specific 'more demanding' framing attributed to Carnival Cruise Lines, 499 U.S. at 595.

Madan supports that mere breach of contract is not a 93A violation and cites Whitinsville Plaza, but it addresses breach of contract rather than a generic 'breach of a legal obligation under commercial law,' so the proposition slightly broadens the holding.

The case applies Massachusetts choice-of-law principles upholding parties' chosen law unless contrary to public policy or another state's fundamental policy under Â§ 187(2), which is related to but not framed as a 'fair and reasonable' standard.

The cited case is Kowalski v. Gagne, but the proposition quotes Law Offices of David Efron v. Matthews & Fullmer Law Firm; although Kowalski contains nearly identical language about judicial notice of other courts' proceedings, it is not the source quoted in the proposition.

Nymark establishes the general rule that a financial institution owes no duty of care to a borrower when acting within the scope of its conventional role as a lender, directly supporting the no-duty proposition.
The excerpt contains the exact quoted language about federal courts taking judicial notice of proceedings in other courts when relevant, directly supporting the proposition.
The opinion explicitly states courts are not required to accept allegations that contradict exhibits attached to the complaint, directly supporting the proposition.
The excerpt covers offense conduct, sentencing procedure, and the Court Interpreters Act issue, with no discussion of Rule 404(b) or the intrinsic-evidence doctrine.

The excerpt shows TRIA Â§ 201(a) authorizes execution against blocked assets and discusses jurisdiction over instrumentalities; it contains no holding that Â§ 201 does not provide for execution against non-blocked assets.

The case states ambiguities are construed against removal because the removal statute is strictly construed in favor of remand, which closely parallels but does not use the exact 'must resolve any ambiguities in favor of remand' phrasing.
The Third Circuit in Lloyd held that Â§ 3 of the FAA mandates a stayâ€”not dismissalâ€”when a party requests one and the claims are arbitrable, directly supporting the proposition.

The opinion expressly distinguishes hazardous wastes (Subtitle C) from nonhazardous solid wastes (Subtitle D) and treats hazardous wastes as a regulated subset of solid wastes, directly supporting the proposition.
The case addresses waiver of issues not raised in the opening brief or below, not issues raised for the first time at oral argument.

TwoRivers expressly holds that federal law determines when a Â§ 1983 claim accrues and that a claim accrues when the plaintiff knows or has reason to know of the injury, directly supporting the proposition.
Interval Licensing held the term-of-degree claim language indefinite; the quoted language about 'unreasonable precision' and 'verbal precision' comes from Nautilus/other cases, not this opinion.
The excerpt addresses lost-profits damages under Â§ 284, not the right-to-exclude as the essential attribute of a patent grant; no on-point passage supports the proposition.
Getty Oil is a foundational Fifth Circuit case on the unanimity rule, requiring each defendant to timely and unambiguously consent to removal.
The opinion expressly states that the Constitution conferred power on Congress to grant exclusive privileges to inventors, deeming such policy nationally important.
The provided excerpt addresses likelihood of success on infringement and nonobviousness, not irreparable harm or marketing/exclusive-market-leader testimony, so it does not support the proposition.
The opinion repeatedly states the abatement doctrine applies when a defendant dies while a direct appeal as of right is pending, directly supporting the proposition.

This case dismisses a habeas appeal for lack of a certificate of probable cause; it does not involve dismissing a lawsuit for lack of subject-matter jurisdiction as a thinly veiled collateral attack on a conviction in another federal district.
The court dismissed the RICO claim and then dismissed the state law claims for lack of supplemental jurisdiction, the opposite of exercising supplemental jurisdiction over them.
Footnote 3 supports the misdemeanor half of the proposition (every circuit rejects an in-presence requirement for misdemeanors), but the case does not address the felony half.
The case vacates the conviction for abuse of discretion in admitting other-acts evidence; it does not uphold admission based on the court limiting prejudicial details.

The case holds conditional statements like 'take these handcuffs off and I'll kick your fucking ass' can be threats, supporting the proposition in substance, but it does not articulate the quoted rule about conditional threats under 'the Code' and relies on a different analytical framing.
Lacey discusses amended complaints superseding originals but the excerpt does not clearly address Rule 15(a)(1)'s 21-day amendment-as-of-right window after a 12(b)/(e)/(f) motion.
The case affirmed dismissal because pro se relators are statutorily barred from bringing FCA qui tam actions, not for failure to prosecute or noncompliance with an order to hire counsel.

The excerpt is a Lanham Act false advertising case about Mylanta antacid; it contains no discussion of laches, delay, or diligent pursuit of avenues over three years before filing a preliminary injunction motion.

The excerpt addresses recusal, sufficiency of evidence, and drug conspiracy facts; it contains no discussion of safeguards against unfair prejudice such as limiting Government elicitation or precautionary limiting instructions.
Goodwin reaffirms that a prosecutor may not punish a defendant for exercising a protected statutory or constitutional right, directly supporting the proposition.
Energizer holds that antecedent basis can be present by implication and lack of explicit antecedent does not render a claim indefinite, directly supporting the proposition.
The excerpt does not contain the quoted definition of 'discard' or any citation to Ecological Rights Foundation; it addresses whether diesel emissions constitute 'disposal' under RCRA.
Porat affirmed denial of leave to amend based on counseled plaintiff's failure to show how defects could be cured, not on a time-barred claim or futility of amendment.

The case discusses California's discovery rule but frames it as triggered by suspicion of wrongdoing, not the 'discovers or has reason to discover' formulation quoted in the proposition, so support is partial.
The Third Circuit affirmed the district court's denial of leave to amend a time-barred claim because the amendment would have been futile, directly matching the proposition.

The Second Circuit in Arista Records v. Doe 3 approved the district court's case management order permitting limited ISP discovery to identify potential infringers as a sensible docket-management approach.
The excerpt shows the D.C. Circuit reversing the district court for abuse of discretion, not endorsing the case management order as a 'sensible way to manage its dockets.'
The opinion expressly states that Rule 15(d) supplemental pleading 'cannot be used to introduce a separate, distinct and new cause of action,' directly supporting the proposition.

The case applies the same-transaction test holding that all claims arising from one core of operative factsâ€”including different legal theoriesâ€”must be brought in the initial action or be barred by res judicata.
Von Saher discusses California's discovery rule under Â§ 338, postponing accrual until the plaintiff discovers or should have discovered the cause of action.
The case addresses waiver of issues raised for the first time in a reply brief, not issues raised for the first time at oral argument.
The opinion contains the exact quoted language and applies it to decline vacating the judgment where substantial rights were unaffected.

Moran holds plaintiff's testimony alone creates a genuine issue of material fact defeating summary judgment, but it cites Anderson v. Mt. Clemens Pottery only for the employer's recordkeeping duty, not as the standard for sufficiency of testimony about hours worked.
The case directly quotes and applies Section 2675(b)'s rule that FTCA claimants cannot recover more than their administrative claim except based on newly discovered evidence or intervening facts.

The excerpt only cites Anderson v. Liberty Lobby for summary judgment standards and does not address Anderson v. Mt. Clemens Pottery's rule that personal-knowledge testimony of hours worked is sufficient evidence.

The case discusses Younger abstention in the context of a Â§ 2241 habeas petition and confirms Younger applies, but it actually held the irreparable harm exception applied and reversed abstention, so it only loosely supports the proposition that Younger hurdles must be satisfied before habeas relief.
The opinion expressly states that upon default, the factual allegations of the complaint, except those relating to damages, will be taken as true.
This case addresses pre-trial habeas custody via detainer under Â§ 2241, not the general definition of 'custody' as encompassing restrictions on liberty from a criminal conviction.
The excerpt does not contain the quoted proposition's language defining adverse employment action; that framing is not articulated in this portion of the opinion.
The Fifth Circuit expressly held the ALJ erred by failing to determine whether Watson could maintain employment as required by Singletary.

The opinion addresses jurisdiction over restitution modification and Â§ 2255 custody limits, not the general definition of 'custody' as encompassing most liberty restrictions from a criminal conviction.

The opinion expressly states Maryland's rule that an insurer must defend whenever the underlying complaint reveals claims even potentially covered, with doubts resolved against the insurerâ€”directly supporting the proposition.
The opinion concerns judicial immunity and enjoining vexatious litigation; it does not address the self-contradiction of granting IFP leave to appeal a complaint dismissed as frivolous.

Pulera holds that withholding requested prescription medications based on legitimate medical concerns (possible abuse/overdose risk) was objectively reasonable, directly supporting the proposition that declining desired medication is not per se unreasonable.
The case addresses Title VII retaliation and pleading legal theories, not the proposition that courts start and end with one discernable legal theory when a plausible claim exists.

Lockett applies the rule that disagreements over medication choice do not establish deliberate indifference absent a substantial departure from accepted professional judgment, directly supporting the proposition.

The Seventh Circuit explicitly reaffirms that granting leave to appeal in forma pauperis from a frivolous-suit dismissal is 'presumptively erroneous and indeed self-contradictory,' directly supporting the proposition.

Lujan expressly contrasts the pleading-stage motion to dismiss standard (general allegations suffice) with the summary judgment standard (must set forth specific facts by affidavit or other evidence).
The case directly holds an insurer's duty to defend exists unless the underlying complaint's allegations clearly fall outside policy coverage, matching the proposition.

The case held Eleventh Amendment immunity barred Â§ 1983 damages against SUNY and officials in their official capacities, but allowed individual-capacity claims and prospective reinstatement to proceed, so immunity did not bar all such claims as the proposition suggests.

The case holds that a non-responding party waives the right to controvert facts and the court should accept properly supported facts as true, which supports the proposition's spirit, though the case's main holding emphasizes that summary judgment still requires the movant meet its Rule 56(c) burden.
Snipes holds that a prisoner's mere disagreement with the course of medical treatment does not constitute an Eighth Amendment deliberate indifference claim, directly supporting the proposition.
The opinion expressly states under Illinois law that the duty to defend arises if facts alleged in the underlying complaint fall within or potentially within the policy's coverage.
The case directly holds that when a non-movant fails to controvert the movant's facts under Rule 56.1, those facts are assumed admitted without controversy.

The opinion holds the ALJ's reliance on a hypothetical that ignored the claimant's credible pain and limitations was improper, directly supporting the proposition that an ALJ cannot pose a hypothetical and disregard unfavorable answers based on substantial evidence.
The opinion explicitly states under Illinois law the duty to defend arises if facts alleged in the underlying complaint fall within or potentially within the policy's coverage.
Stringer addresses Article III standing requirements for injunctive relief based on future injury risk; the excerpt contains no holding that worry or anxiety fail to qualify as concrete injury.

Pinkston's refusal of the x-ray and refusal to be seen by the doctor were cited as undermining his deliberate indifference claim, supporting that a prisoner's refusal of treatment defeats deliberate indifference.
The opinion affirms denials of IFP status and discusses abuse of discretion; it does not address any self-contradiction in granting IFP appeal of a complaint dismissed as frivolous.

The Seventh Circuit explicitly states the presumption that when all federal claims are dismissed before trial, the district court will relinquish jurisdiction over supplemental state-law claims, directly supporting the proposition.
The opinion expressly recognizes 'a range of acceptable courses' for prison medical treatment under the Eighth Amendment, directly supporting the proposition.
McKenna explicitly rejects the release/covenant-not-to-sue distinction as 'entirely artificial,' so it does not support the proposition.

Toguchi holds that a mere difference of medical opinion is insufficient and requires showing the chosen treatment was medically unacceptable, which supports the proposition's substance, but the specific 'range of acceptable courses' phrasing traces to Jackson v. McIntosh rather than Toguchi's own language.
The opinion expressly states that courts compare work history and company policies, including disciplinary records, in determining whether employees are similarly situated.

The case holds prison officials were not deliberately indifferent where Walker refused the HIV test prerequisite to treatment, which supports the proposition's spirit, but the court's reasoning focused on the reasonableness of requiring the test rather than a clean rule that patient refusal categorically defeats deliberate indifference.

The case discusses Rule 12(b)(6) standards and the conversion to summary judgment when extrinsic materials are considered, but it does not directly contrast the substantive standards of the two motions.

Mickey supports causal inference from temporal proximity (termination day employer learned of EEOC charge), but the proposition's specific framing about handing a supervisor a note of discrimination allegations does not match the EEOC-charge facts here.
The opinion expressly cites Sawtelle, 70 F.3d at 1390-91, and C.W. Downer, 771 F.3d at 65, for the personal-jurisdiction framework, matching the proposition's cited pages.
